title: What the electoral bonds ruling actually changed
p1:
Most people think the electoral bonds ruling was about banning political donations.

It wasn't.

What changed was something far more fundamental: secrecy.
p2:
A common misconception is that the Supreme Court stopped companies and individuals from funding political parties.

Political donations are still allowed.

The major shift was that the electoral bonds system, which allowed donations to remain hidden from the public, was struck down. The Court said voters have a right to know who is funding political parties because that information can influence how people evaluate political decisions.
p3:
The practical lesson goes beyond politics.

The ruling highlights the difference between allowing an activity and allowing it to happen without transparency.

Many debates are framed as "Should this be permitted?" But an equally important question is "Who gets to see what's happening?"

One of the biggest outcomes was the disclosure of donor and recipient data that had previously been shielded from public view.

That's the part many people missed.

The ruling was less about stopping money and more about increasing visibility around it.
conclusion: Do you think transparency about political funding changes how citizens evaluate parties and public policy decisions?
